questions.question: The Salem Witch Trials of 1692 resulted in the deaths of 20 people in colonial Massachusetts. What was the primary method of execution used?
questions.explanation: Contrary to popular belief, those condemned in Salem were hanged, not burned at the stake. The one exception was Giles Corey, who was pressed to death under heavy stones after refusing to enter a plea. Nineteen people were hanged on Gallows Hill between June and September 1692.
questions.memory_hook: Salem hanged its victims, didn't burn them — Giles Corey alone was pressed to death with stones for refusing to enter a plea.

questions.question: The Seneca Falls Convention of 1848 launched the American women's rights movement. Which two women are primarily credited with organizing it?
options: Susan B. Anthony and Sojourner Truth
Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Lucretia Mott
Harriet Tubman and Ida B. Wells
questions.answer: Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Lucretia Mott
questions.explanation: Stanton and Quaker activist Lucretia Mott organized the convention after being excluded from the 1840 World Anti-Slavery Convention in London. Their Declaration of Sentiments, modeled on the Declaration of Independence, declared 'all men and women are created equal' and demanded voting rights for women — a goal not achieved until the 19th Amendment in 1920.
questions.memory_hook: Stanton and Mott launched women's rights at Seneca Falls 1848 — their Declaration of Sentiments echoed Jefferson but added women, 72 years before the vote was won.

questions.question: Around 1000 AD, Norse explorer Leif Erikson established a settlement in North America — nearly 500 years before Columbus. What did the Norse name this land?
questions.explanation: Leif Erikson's settlement at L'Anse aux Meadows in present-day Newfoundland, Canada is the only confirmed pre-Columbian European settlement in the Americas, proven by archaeological excavations beginning in 1960. The Norse called it Vinland, likely for wild grapes or berries found there.
questions.memory_hook: Leif Erikson reached Vinland (Newfoundland) around 1000 AD — L'Anse aux Meadows ruins confirmed it, 500 years before Columbus sailed.

questions.question: At the 1972 Munich Olympics, swimmer Mark Spitz set a record that stood for 36 years. What was his historic achievement?
options: Won 7 gold medals, setting a world record in every event
Became the first swimmer to break the 1-minute barrier in the 100m freestyle
Won gold in four consecutive Olympic Games
Set 10 individual world records in a single calendar year
questions.answer: Won 7 gold medals, setting a world record in every event
questions.explanation: Spitz won 7 gold medals at Munich 1972, breaking the world record in each of the seven events — a feat unmatched until Michael Phelps won 8 golds at Beijing 2008. The triumph was overshadowed by the murder of 11 Israeli athletes and coaches by Palestinian terrorists during the same Games.
questions.memory_hook: Spitz's 7-for-7 gold-and-world-record sweep at Munich 1972 stood for 36 years until Phelps took 8 golds at Beijing 2008.

questions.question: During Argentina's 1986 World Cup quarter-final win over England, Diego Maradona scored two of the most famous goals in soccer history in the same match. What was his first, controversial goal called?
questions.explanation: Maradona punched the ball into the net with his left fist but the referee allowed the goal. He later described it as scored 'a little with the head of Maradona and a little with the hand of God.' Just four minutes later he scored what FIFA voters named the Goal of the Century — a solo run past five defenders from his own half.
questions.memory_hook: Same match, four minutes apart: Maradona cheated with his hand ('Hand of God'), then scored what FIFA called the Goal of the Century past five defenders.

questions.question: Steven Spielberg's 1993 blockbuster Jurassic Park pioneered photo-realistic CGI. On which author's novel was it based?
questions.explanation: Michael Crichton's 1990 novel used chaos theory — voiced through mathematician Ian Malcolm — to argue that re-creating dinosaurs from ancient DNA preserved in amber was inherently unstable and doomed to fail. Spielberg's film used a revolutionary blend of CGI and practical animatronics that transformed visual effects filmmaking.
questions.memory_hook: Crichton's chaos-theory novel became Spielberg's CGI revolution — Ian Malcolm's warnings that life 'finds a way' proved right both in the story and in Hollywood.

questions.question: Stanley Kubrick's 1964 dark comedy Dr. Strangelove satirized Cold War nuclear policy. What is the film's complete subtitle?
options: Or: How I Learned to Stop Worrying and Love the Bomb
Or: The Madness of Mutual Assured Destruction
Or: The Last War We Will Ever Fight
questions.answer: Or: How I Learned to Stop Worrying and Love the Bomb
questions.explanation: Kubrick adapted Peter George's serious nuclear-war novel Red Alert into black comedy after realizing the premise was inherently absurd. Peter Sellers played three roles — the US President, RAF officer Mandrake, and the title character — while George C. Scott played a bellicose general. The film remains the definitive satire of nuclear deterrence.
questions.memory_hook: Kubrick turned nuclear annihilation into comedy because the MAD doctrine was too absurd to play straight — Peter Sellers played three roles to drive the point home.

questions.question: In Back to the Future (1985), Marty McFly travels through time in a modified DeLorean. What speed must the car reach to activate time travel?
questions.explanation: Writer-director Robert Zemeckis chose 88 mph because the DeLorean's digital speedometer looked striking at that number. The car also required 1.21 gigawatts of power, originally supplied by a plutonium-powered reactor and later by a lightning bolt. The DeLorean DMC-12's gull-wing doors added to its futuristic appearance.
questions.memory_hook: 88 mph and 1.21 gigawatts activates the DeLorean's flux capacitor — numbers chosen because they looked great on the car's digital dash.

questions.question: The Baltic Sea has dramatically lower salinity than typical ocean water. What primarily explains this?
options: High freshwater river inflow and low evaporation in a cool climate
Extensive underground freshwater springs on the seafloor
It was entirely freshwater until 8,000 years ago and is still transitioning
questions.answer: High freshwater river inflow and low evaporation in a cool climate
questions.explanation: Rivers across northern Europe pour massive volumes of freshwater into the Baltic, while its cool climate reduces evaporation. Its connection to the Atlantic through the narrow Danish Straits limits saltwater exchange. The Baltic's salinity averages about 7–8 parts per thousand — roughly a quarter that of the open ocean — and it nearly freezes in winter in its northern reaches.
questions.memory_hook: The Baltic is nearly fresh — rivers pour in, cold air cuts evaporation, and the narrow Danish Straits barely let salty Atlantic water mix in.

questions.question: The Orinoco, one of South America's great rivers, flows through which two countries before emptying into the Atlantic?
questions.explanation: The Orinoco flows roughly 2,140 km through Venezuela, with its upper reaches forming part of the border with Colombia. A remarkable natural feature is the Casiquiare canal, a natural waterway that connects the Orinoco basin to the Amazon basin — one of the few places on Earth where two major river systems naturally interconnect.
questions.memory_hook: The Orinoco flows through Venezuela and Colombia, and uniquely connects to the Amazon via the natural Casiquiare waterway.

questions.question: In quantum mechanics, the double-slit experiment reveals a paradox about particles like electrons and photons. What does the experiment demonstrate?
options: Particles travel faster than light when unobserved
Particles show wave-like interference patterns when unobserved but behave as particles when measured
Particles can communicate instantly across any distance
Particles reverse direction when two slits are opened simultaneously
questions.answer: Particles show wave-like interference patterns when unobserved but behave as particles when measured
questions.explanation: When electrons pass through two slits without being observed, they create an interference pattern on a screen — proof of wave behavior. When a detector is added to determine which slit an electron passes through, the interference pattern disappears and particles behave as discrete objects. This 'observer effect' challenges classical notions of reality and remains central to quantum mechanics.
questions.memory_hook: The double-slit experiment: unobserved electrons make wave interference patterns, but act like solid particles the instant you try to watch — observation changes the outcome.

questions.question: Many animals survive winter through hibernation. How does a hibernating black bear's body temperature and heart rate change compared to its active state?
options: Temperature drops to near freezing; heart rate falls to 2–3 beats per minute
Temperature drops about 10–12°F; heart rate falls from ~55 to 8–19 beats per minute
Temperature and heart rate stay normal but the bear becomes immobile
Temperature rises to conserve core organ function; heart rate doubles
questions.answer: Temperature drops about 10–12°F; heart rate falls from ~55 to 8–19 beats per minute
questions.explanation: Bears are classified as 'light hibernators' — their body temperature drops only 10–12°F (versus near-freezing in true hibernators like ground squirrels). Heart rate falls from a normal ~55 bpm to as low as 8–19 bpm. Unlike deep hibernators, bears can rouse relatively quickly, which is why disturbing a denning bear is dangerous.
questions.memory_hook: Bears are light hibernators — just a 10°F temperature drop and single-digit heart rates, but they wake far faster than ground squirrels whose temperatures plunge near freezing.
